1: 'See, I have made you as God (elohim) to Pharaoh': The divine commission sets the epistemological frame for the plague narrative. In Egyptian theology, Pharaoh was not merely a human king but the divine son of Ra, the living Horus, god incarnate — the absolute divine-human mediator. By making Moses 'as God to Pharaoh,' YHWH posits a counter-authority to Pharaoh's divine claim: the God of the Hebrew slaves outranks the god-king of Egypt. This verse establishes the plague cycle as a theological duel between competing divine claims, not merely a political liberation movement.
2: Aaron your brother shall tell Pharaoh to let the people of Israel go: The Moses-Aaron spokesman chain mirrors the Egyptian royal court structure in which a herald (wpwty) announced decrees on behalf of the pharaoh. Aaron functions as Moses's wpwty before Pharaoh's court. The Egyptian royal court was an elaborate ceremonial institution in which direct access to Pharaoh was rare; approaching through a herald/spokesman was the normal protocol for formal audiences.
3: I will harden Pharaoh's heart, and though I multiply my signs (otot) and wonders (moftim) in the land of Egypt: The otot (signs) and moftim (portents/wonders) vocabulary is the standard ANE language for acts of divine power that demonstrate a deity's sovereignty; Egyptian royal ideology used similar vocabulary for the pharaoh's military and cultic accomplishments — carved on temple walls as evidence of divine mandate. YHWH's signs directly compete with the pharaonic claims. The Merneptah Stele (c. 1208 BCE) uses similar triumph language: gods demonstrated their power through chosen kings.
4: Pharaoh will not listen to you. Then I will lay my hand on Egypt and bring out my hosts (tzivotai), my people the children of Israel, by great acts of judgment: Tzivotai = my armies/hosts (from tzava = army; the same root as tzabaot in 'YHWH of Hosts'); Israel is YHWH's army, organized for departure as a military force. The 'hand of YHWH' (yad YHWH) is both an idiom for divine power and a military metaphor — the god who strikes with his 'hand' in battle. Egyptian royal inscriptions describe the pharaoh's victories as the 'strong arm' (hand) of Amun or Ra.
5: The Egyptians shall know that I am YHWH (yede'u Mitzrayim ki ani YHWH) when I stretch out my hand over Egypt: The da'at formula ('shall know that I am YHWH') appears seven times in chapters 7-14 — a deliberate sevenfold pattern. Egypt's knowledge of YHWH is the stated divine purpose of the plagues. The Egyptian concept of knowledge of a deity (rx) was equally important; to know the true name and power of a deity was the basis of religious authority. YHWH is asserting that Egypt will come to know a superior divine power through the plagues.
6: Moses and Aaron did so; as YHWH commanded them, so they did: The compliance formula (ka'asher tzivah YHWH... ken asu) appears throughout chapters 36-40 for the tabernacle construction and here at the start of the plague cycle — the same language of complete obedience. The plague narrative is framed as executed instructions, not improvised responses.
7: Moses was eighty years old, and Aaron eighty-three years old, when they spoke to Pharaoh: The ages situate Moses and Aaron in the generational context: they belong to the generation born under the initial oppression (after Pharaoh's death decree for Hebrew male infants). Moses's 40 years in Midian (Acts 7:23,30 supplies the 40+40 framework) + 40 in Egypt = 80 at the Exodus. The age information is unusual in narrative contexts — it emphasizes that these are not young men but seasoned lives being used by YHWH for this specific moment.
8: Then YHWH said to Moses and Aaron, 'When Pharaoh says to you, Give us a sign (mofet), then you shall say to Aaron, Take your staff': The demand for a mofet (portent/sign) was standard in Egyptian royal audiences — a claimant to divine authority was expected to demonstrate it through a miraculous sign. Egyptian texts describe various signs performed by priests and magicians. The serpent sign was chosen deliberately: the uraeus serpent (the cobra worn on Pharaoh's crown) was the symbol of royal divine authority. Aaron's staff-turned-serpent challenges Pharaoh's own emblem of divine power.
9: Aaron's staff became a serpent (nachash): The word nachash (serpent) connects to the Eden serpent (Gen 3) and to the bronze serpent of Numbers 21. The Egyptian word for the royal cobra on Pharaoh's crown was 'iaret' (uraeus); the cobra was sacred to Wadjet, the protective goddess of Lower Egypt. Aaron's serpent represents a counter-claim to the cobra-power of the Egyptian crown. The transformation of a staff into a snake was also known in Egyptian magical literature — the magicians' ability to replicate it means the sign was within the range of known Egyptian magic.
10: Moses and Aaron went to Pharaoh and did just as YHWH commanded: The royal audience with Pharaoh takes place in a context of high Egyptian ceremony. The court of the pharaoh was among the most elaborate bureaucratic and ceremonial institutions in the ancient world; entering it required protocol. Moses and Aaron stand before a court that considers itself the center of the cosmos — the son of Ra surrounded by his divine court officials.
11: Then Pharaoh also called the wise men and the sorcerers, and they also, the magicians (chartumim) of Egypt, did the same with their secret arts (lehateichem): Chartumim = the Hebrew transliteration of the Egyptian 'kher-heb' (chief ritual lector priests — the priests who recited ritual texts from papyrus scrolls during religious ceremonies). These were not carnival tricksters but the highest-ranking ritual specialists in Egyptian religion. In 2 Timothy 3:8 they are named as Jannes and Jambres (a tradition preserved in later Jewish texts including the Damascus Document). Lahat (secret arts/occult craft) is the same word used for the flaming sword guarding Eden (Genesis 3:24).
12: For each man cast down his staff and they became serpents. But Aaron's staff swallowed up their staffs: The magicians could replicate the sign — but Aaron's serpent ate theirs. The swallowing (bala') is the decisive indicator: YHWH's representative does not merely match the Egyptian priests but surpasses and consumes their power. The competitive swallowing may reflect the Egyptian cosmological concept of divine power establishing supremacy by consuming a rival deity's manifestation.
13: Still Pharaoh's heart was hardened, and he would not listen to them, as YHWH had said: Pharaoh interprets the result favorably for his own side — the magicians matched the sign, so the overall event is a draw that justifies maintaining his position. This is the first of 10 hardenings in chapters 7-14. The hardening of Pharaoh's heart is both a theological description (YHWH's sovereignty over history) and a psychological description (Pharaoh's own confirmation bias).
14: Then YHWH said to Moses, 'Pharaoh's heart is heavy (kaveid):' Kaveid (heavy) is the Qal stative of kavad (the same root as kavod = glory). Pharaoh's heavy heart is the anti-glory — the weight that refuses to respond to the divine summons. The Egyptians believed the heart (ib) was weighed by Anubis after death against the feather of Ma'at (truth/justice); a heart too heavy with sin failed the judgment. Pharaoh's kaveid heart anticipates its own eschatological verdict.
15: Go to Pharaoh in the morning, as he is going out to the water: Pharaoh's morning visit to the Nile was likely a religious ritual — the pharaoh participated in daily worship ceremonies, and the Nile held special cultic significance. Egyptian inscriptions describe pharaohs performing rites at the Nile's edge. The instruction to intercept Pharaoh at the water is a deliberate choice of venue: the confrontation happens at the Nile, which is the first target of the plague.
16: 'The LORD, the God of the Hebrews, has sent me to you, saying, Let my people go, that they may serve me in the wilderness:' The repeated identification 'YHWH God of the Hebrews' (YHWH elohei ha-Ivrim) defines YHWH as the deity specifically associated with the Hebrew people — from Egypt's perspective, a tribal deity of slave laborers, not a universal sovereign. The plagues systematically demonstrate that this 'tribal deity' controls every dimension of Egyptian reality.
17: 'I will strike the water of the Nile (y'or) with the staff, and it shall turn to blood:' Y'or = the Nile (the Egyptian word 'Itrw' = the River, borrowed into Hebrew as y'or; the Nile is never called a river by its generic name but always y'or in Exodus). The Nile was Egypt's lifeline — the source of water, food, transportation, and the annual flood that fertilized the fields. Striking the Nile was striking Egypt's foundational life-support system. The blood transformation targeted Hapy (the Nile-flood deity of abundance), Khnum (creator god and guardian of the Nile's sources at the First Cataract/Elephantine), and Osiris (in some traditions the Nile itself was Osiris's blood flowing through Egypt).
18: 'The fish in the Nile shall die, and the Nile shall stink, and the Egyptians will grow weary of drinking water from the Nile:' Fish were a major protein source for Egyptians of all classes; Egyptian art frequently depicts fishing in the Nile. The death of the fish would cause a significant food crisis independent of the water pollution. 'The Egyptians will grow weary (nile'u)' — the same word used in Exodus 7:24 (they dug along the Nile) and in Exodus 18:18 (you will surely wear out). The plague creates a weariness that anticipates eventual capitulation.
19: 'Say to Aaron, Take your staff and stretch out your hand over the waters of Egypt, over their rivers, their canals, and their ponds, and all their pools of water, so that they may become blood:' The comprehensive list (y'orot = Nile branches; ye'orim = canals; agamim = ponds; mikveh ha-mayim = reservoirs/pools) ensures the plague covers Egypt's entire water system. Egypt's hydraulic infrastructure was extensive: the Nile's multiple branches in the Delta, irrigation canals throughout the cultivated land, fish ponds, and storage cisterns — all affected.
20: Moses and Aaron did as YHWH commanded. In the sight of Pharaoh and in the sight of his servants he lifted up the staff and struck the water in the Nile, and all the water in the Nile turned into blood: The public performance of the sign 'in the sight of Pharaoh and his servants' is deliberate. Egyptian inscriptions and tomb paintings regularly describe pharaonic and priestly acts performed before witnesses to validate them. The plague is a public theological demonstration, not a private miracle.
21: And the fish in the Nile died, and the Nile stank, so that the Egyptians could not drink water from the Nile: The Nile's stench would have been catastrophic in a civilization where the Nile's smell was associated with fertility and abundance; the smell of the inundation was culturally positive. The inversion of the Nile's characteristic scent into a stench is a sensory reversal of Egypt's fundamental relationship with its river. Ancient Egyptians described the Nile flood as 'the perfume of the gods' — now it smells of death.
22: But the magicians of Egypt did the same by their secret arts. So Pharaoh's heart remained hardened, and he would not listen to them, as YHWH had said: The magicians replicating the blood plague is often cited as evidence of limited occult power — but the theological point is that their replication made the situation worse (more blood, not less). They could not reverse the plague; they could only add to it. Pharaoh uses their replication as cover for continued refusal. Egyptian records do describe magical rituals involving blood symbolism in apotropaic contexts.
23: Pharaoh turned and went into his house, and he did not take even this to heart: The Egyptian idiom for unresponsiveness to a divine sign would have been understood as a failure of ma'at — pharaoh's obligation to maintain cosmic order required responsiveness to divine communications. Pharaoh's dismissal of YHWH's sign is simultaneously a failure of his theological obligation and an act of political defiance.
24: And all the Egyptians dug along the Nile for water to drink, for they could not drink the water of the Nile: Archaeological evidence shows the ancient Egyptians were highly skilled at finding groundwater; shallow wells in the Nile basin floor could yield potable water even when the surface water was polluted. The Egyptians' ingenuity in bypassing the plague (digging for filtered groundwater) is consistent with the practical resourcefulness shown throughout Egyptian history in managing their water resources.
25: Seven full days passed after YHWH had struck the Nile: The seven-day duration of the first plague before the second begins is the only explicit plague duration given in the narrative. Seven days = one week = the complete creation unit. The plague lasted long enough to be taken seriously but ended before it permanently destroyed Egypt's water system. The temporal structure of the plagues (plague → hardening → relief → new plague) creates a rhythmic escalation that gives Pharaoh repeated opportunities to respond.
